quotes.verbatim_quote:
A red circle represents the root (aka 1) of the chord. Most chords in this dictionary
have a C root.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the visual encoding of the tonic/root in every diagram in the book, establishing the reference pitch all chord tones are measured against.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The black circles are the other notes of the chord. The numbers inside the black
circles indicate the chord tone.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains that fingering diagrams display harmonic function (scale-degree intervals) rather than finger numbers, which is essential for reading every chord entry correctly.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The numbers (1, 2, 3, and 4) at the right side of each chord diagram indicate which
fingers to use to fret the chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This locates the left-hand fingering indicators and distinguishes them from the chord-tone numbers inside the circles, preventing the most common diagram-reading confusion.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The X symbol at the left side of the chord diagram indicates that those strings are not
to be played. This means that most of the chords on this page cannot be strummed,
but should be played fingerstyle, with your thumb and first 3 fingers of the right hand.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a technique rule that governs right-hand approach for the majority of chords in the book — fingerstyle with thumb + 3 fingers rather than strumming.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The O symbol at the left side of the chord diagram indicates that those strings are
played open.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the open-string symbol, completing the set of string-status indicators needed to interpret any diagram in the dictionary.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Most of the chords on this page are of the movable type (not the open chords though).
By moving the chord shapes up or down the guitar neck, you get other chords of the
same chord type.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the book's core transposition principle: every non-open shape is a template that can be slid to any root, multiplying the dictionary's reach across all twelve keys.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
For example: move the chord shape of a Cmaj7 voicing 2 frets higher and it becomes a
Dmaj7 voicing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This concrete example demonstrates the two-fret = whole-step relationship that underpins all transposition of movable shapes in the book.
